questions.question:
Humans have the ability to construct worlds entirely in their minds, which don't exist in the physical world. So far as we know, no other species possesses this ability. This skill is so important that we have different words to refer to its different flavors, such as imagination, invention and innovation. Artificial intelligence (AI) is currently a hot topic. AI enables computers to learn, given enough training data. For humans, sitting in front of a computer for long hours can lead to health issues.

Based on the above passage, which one of the following is TRUE?

A. No species possess the ability to construct worlds in their minds
B. The terms imagination, invention and innovation are not related skills
C. We do not know of any species other than humans who possess the ability to construct mental worlds
D. Imagination, invention and innovation are unrelated to the ability to construct mental worlds
questions.answer: C. We do not know of any species other than humans who possess the ability to construct mental worlds
questions.to_find: Which statement is TRUE based on the passage about human ability to construct mental worlds

understand.plain: The passage states that humans uniquely possess the ability to construct non-physical worlds in their minds, and no other species is known to have this ability. Words like imagination, invention, and innovation describe different aspects of this single skill. The passage also mentions AI enabling computers to learn and that long computer use can cause health issues.

keywords.explain: The human ability to imagine and mentally create scenarios, objects, or realities that do not physically exist
keywords.example: An architect mentally visualising a building before drawing it

keywords.explain: A qualifier meaning 'based on current knowledge', implying the statement is the best available understanding, not an absolute certainty
keywords.example: Scientists say: so far as we know, no planet in our solar system supports life

keywords.explain: Different varieties or forms of the same underlying ability; here imagination, invention and innovation are all variations of the same mental-world-building skill
keywords.example: Sweet, sour and bitter are different flavors of taste, but all are forms of taste

understand.visual_alt: A reading comprehension passage about human mental world-building and AI

given.aim: Identify the statement that is true according to the passage

terms.meaning: Humans can construct worlds in their minds that don't exist physically
terms.connects: Directly supports or refutes each answer option

terms.meaning: No other species (so far as we know) possesses this ability
terms.example: Distinguishes humans from all other known species
terms.connects: Matches the wording of option C precisely

terms.meaning: Imagination, invention and innovation are different words for flavors of the same skill
terms.connects: Directly contradicts option D and option B

terms.meaning: Option A says no species can construct worlds — but the passage says humans can, so A is false
terms.example: The passage explicitly says humans have this ability

given.plan: Match each answer option to claims made in the passage; the correct answer must be directly supported by what the passage explicitly states

steps.title: Eliminate option A
steps.apply: Option A says no species possesses this ability. The passage says humans do possess it. So A is FALSE.
steps.note: A direct contradiction with the opening sentence of the passage

steps.title: Eliminate option B
steps.apply: Option B says imagination, invention and innovation are not related skills. The passage says they are different flavors (varieties) of the same skill. So B is FALSE.
steps.note: Passage treats them as related manifestations of one ability

steps.title: Eliminate option D
steps.apply: Option D claims those three terms are unrelated to constructing mental worlds. The passage explicitly introduces them as words for different flavors of exactly that ability. So D is FALSE.
steps.note: The passage directly links the three terms to mental world construction

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: Option C says: we do not know of any species other than humans who possess this ability. The passage states: 'So far as we know, no other species possesses this ability.' Option C accurately paraphrases this claim.
steps.note: The qualifier 'so far as we know' maps exactly to 'we do not know of any other species'

solution.result: Option C is TRUE: we do not know of any species other than humans who possess the ability to construct mental worlds.

questions.formula_note: This is a reading comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required. Logical elimination of false options confirms option C.

questions.question:
The author said, "Musicians rehearse before their concerts. Actors rehearse their roles before the opening of a new play. On the other hand, I find it strange that many public speakers think they can just walk onto the stage and start speaking. In my opinion, it is no less important for public speakers to rehearse their talks."

Based on the above passage, which one of the following is TRUE?

A. The author is of the opinion that rehearsing is important for musicians, public speakers and actors
B. The author is of the opinion that rehearsing is less important for public speakers than for musicians and actors
C. The author is of the opinion that rehearsing is more important for musicians than for public speakers
D. The author is of the opinion that rehearsing for a talk is more important for actors than for musicians and public speakers
questions.answer: A. The author is of the opinion that rehearsing is important for musicians, public speakers and actors
questions.to_find: Which statement is TRUE about the author's opinion on rehearsing

understand.plain: The author observes that musicians and actors routinely rehearse before performances. The author finds it strange that public speakers often skip rehearsal, implying they are wrong not to rehearse. The key phrase 'no less important' means equally important — the author believes rehearsing is as necessary for public speakers as it is for musicians and actors.

keywords.explain: A phrase meaning equally important or just as important; it does not mean more or less important, but the same level of importance
keywords.example: 'Sleep is no less important than diet' means sleep is as important as diet

keywords.explain: To practice or prepare a performance or talk in advance before the actual event
keywords.example: A band playing through songs the night before their concert

keywords.explain: A contrast connector introducing a contrasting or contradictory situation
keywords.example: Used here to contrast musicians/actors (who rehearse) with public speakers (who often do not)

understand.visual_alt: A reading passage about the importance of rehearsal for musicians, actors, and public speakers

given.aim: Determine which option correctly reflects the author's stated opinion about rehearsal

terms.meaning: Musicians rehearse before concerts — stated as an accepted positive practice
terms.example: Implied: rehearsal is important for musicians
terms.connects: Establishes musicians as a baseline for rehearsal importance

terms.meaning: Actors rehearse roles before plays — also stated as an accepted positive practice
terms.example: Implied: rehearsal is important for actors
terms.connects: Establishes actors as another baseline alongside musicians

terms.meaning: Many public speakers skip rehearsal; the author finds this strange and says it is 'no less important' for them to rehearse
terms.example: Rehearsal is equally important for public speakers
terms.connects: The phrase 'no less important' is the critical logical hinge for selecting the correct option

terms.meaning: Rehearsal matters equally for all three groups: musicians, actors, and public speakers
terms.example: If A is no less important than B, then A >= B in importance; combined with no claim of superiority, it means equal
terms.connects: Confirms option A as the only statement consistent with the passage

given.plan: Parse the key phrase 'no less important' precisely and match it against each option; eliminate any option that introduces a hierarchy not stated in the passage

steps.title: Identify the author's core claim
steps.apply: The author explicitly values rehearsal for musicians (concerts) and actors (plays). For public speakers, the author says rehearsing is 'no less important' — meaning equally important. So the author believes rehearsal matters equally for all three groups.
steps.note: The phrase 'no less important' is the key logical phrase

steps.title: Eliminate option B
steps.apply: Option B says rehearsing is less important for public speakers. This directly contradicts 'no less important'. B is FALSE.
steps.note: The author finds it strange that speakers skip rehearsal — implying they should rehearse

steps.title: Eliminate option C
steps.apply: Option C says rehearsal is more important for musicians than for public speakers. The passage makes no such ranking. 'No less important' establishes equality, not inferiority of public speakers. C is FALSE.
steps.note: No comparative ranking between musicians and public speakers is made in the passage

steps.title: Eliminate option D
steps.apply: Option D ranks actors above musicians and public speakers in rehearsal importance. The passage states no such hierarchy. D is FALSE.
steps.note: The author does not rank any group above another

steps.title: Confirm option A
steps.apply: Option A says rehearsing is important for musicians, public speakers, and actors. The passage endorses rehearsal for musicians and actors directly, and argues it is equally important ('no less important') for public speakers. A correctly captures the author's view for all three groups.
steps.note: Option A is the only option consistent with the precise meaning of 'no less important'

solution.result: Option A is TRUE: the author believes rehearsing is important for musicians, public speakers, and actors equally.

questions.formula_note: This is a reading comprehension question relying on precise interpretation of the phrase 'no less important'; no mathematical formulas are needed.

questions.question:
The Canadian constitution requires that equal importance be given to English and French. Last year, Air Canada lost a lawsuit, and had to pay a six-figure fine to a French-speaking couple after they filed complaints about formal in-flight announcements in English lasting 13 seconds, as opposed to informal 5 second messages in French.

The French-speaking couple filed a complaint against Air Canada because

A. the in-flight announcements being made in English
B. the English announcements being longer than the French ones
C. the English announcements being longer than the French ones
D. equal importance being given to both English and French
E. equal importance being given to both English and French

Based on the given passage, which one of the following is the correct inference?

A. the in-flight announcements being made in English
B. the English announcements being longer than the French ones
C. the English announcements being longer than the French ones
D. equal importance being given to both English and French
questions.answer: C. the English announcements being longer than the French ones
questions.to_find: Why the French-speaking couple filed a complaint against Air Canada

understand.plain: The Canadian constitution mandates equal treatment of English and French. The couple complained because Air Canada's formal in-flight announcements in English lasted 13 seconds, while informal French messages were only 5 seconds. This unequal treatment — longer English, shorter French — violated the constitutional requirement of equal importance for both languages.

keywords.explain: The Canadian constitutional requirement that both English and French receive the same level of attention, formality, and duration in official communications
keywords.example: If English announcements are 13 seconds, French announcements must also be approximately 13 seconds

keywords.explain: The distinction between official, structured announcements (formal) and casual, brief messages (informal); formal English at 13 s vs informal French at 5 s shows imbalance in both duration and formality
keywords.example: A formal English safety briefing being more detailed than an informal French translation

keywords.explain: A monetary penalty between 100,000 and 999,999 dollars; indicates the court considered the violation serious
keywords.example: A $100,000 fine is a six-figure amount

understand.visual_alt: A reading comprehension passage about an Air Canada language-equality lawsuit

given.aim: Identify the correct reason why the French-speaking couple filed a complaint

terms.meaning: English and French must be given equal importance in all official communications in Canada
terms.example: Formal announcements must be of equal length and formality in both languages
terms.connects: Sets the legal standard against which Air Canada's announcements are judged

terms.meaning: English formal in-flight announcements were 13 seconds long
terms.connects: Shows English received more time and formality

terms.meaning: French messages were only 5 seconds and were informal
terms.connects: French received less time and lower formality than English — the root of the complaint

terms.meaning: The couple's complaint is about unequal treatment: English got more time (13 s) than French (5 s), violating equal-importance requirement
terms.example: 13 seconds > 5 seconds: English announcements were longer than French ones
terms.connects: Directly maps to the correct answer option

given.plan: Focus on what the passage explicitly says the couple complained about: the disparity in duration and formality between English and French announcements

steps.title: Identify the factual basis of the complaint
steps.apply: The passage says the couple complained about formal English announcements lasting 13 seconds versus informal French messages of only 5 seconds. The disparity is in length (13 s > 5 s) and formality (formal vs informal).
steps.note: Both duration and formality are unequal, but the measurable comparison given is 13 s vs 5 s

steps.title: Eliminate option A
steps.apply: Option A: complaints about announcements being made in English. But French announcements were also made — the complaint is not that English was used, but about the inequality in treatment. A is not the correct inference.
steps.note: The Canadian constitution requires both languages to be used; the issue is equal treatment, not the use of English itself

steps.title: Eliminate option D
steps.apply: Option D: equal importance being given to both languages. This is what the constitution requires, not the couple's complaint. The couple complained because equal importance was NOT being given. D is the opposite of the problem.
steps.note: Option D describes the constitutional standard, not the violation

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: Option C: the English announcements being longer than the French ones. This directly matches the passage: 13 seconds (English, formal) vs 5 seconds (French, informal). The English announcements were longer, violating the equal-importance requirement. C is the correct inference.
steps.note: 13 s > 5 s confirms English was longer; this unequal treatment is the grounds for the complaint

solution.result: Option C is correct: the couple complained because the English announcements (13 seconds, formal) were longer than the French ones (5 seconds, informal), violating the constitutional requirement of equal importance.

questions.formula_note: This is a reading comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required. The inference is based on comparing the stated durations: 13 seconds (English) vs 5 seconds (French).

questions.question:
The global financial crisis of 2008 is considered to be the most serious world-wide financial crisis, which started with the sub-prime lending crisis in USA in 2007. The sub-prime lending crisis led to the banking crisis in 2008 with the collapse of Lehman Brothers in 2008. The sub-prime lending refers to the provision of loans to those borrowers who may have difficulties in repaying loans, and it arises because of excess liquidity following the East Asian crisis.

Which one of the following sequences shows the CORRECT order of events as per the given passage?

A. East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis
B. Sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis -> East Asian crisis
C. Banking crisis -> East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis
D. East Asian crisis -> banking crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis
questions.answer: A. East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis
questions.to_find: The correct chronological sequence of financial crises as described in the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes a chain of financial events. The East Asian crisis created excess liquidity, which gave rise to sub-prime lending. The sub-prime lending crisis (2007, USA) then triggered the global financial crisis (2008), which included the banking crisis marked by the collapse of Lehman Brothers (2008). The question tests whether students can reconstruct this causal chain in the correct order.

keywords.explain: The practice of providing loans to borrowers who are considered high-risk because they may struggle to repay; excess liquidity (from the East Asian crisis) made lenders willing to take this risk
keywords.example: A bank giving a mortgage to someone with poor credit history because money is cheap and plentiful

keywords.explain: An abundance of money in the financial system, making loans cheap and easy to obtain; it originated from the East Asian crisis and fueled risky lending
keywords.example: Low interest rates after the East Asian crisis made credit very accessible globally

keywords.explain: A sequence where each event directly causes the next; here: East Asian crisis -> excess liquidity -> sub-prime lending -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis
keywords.example: Dominos falling: pushing the first one causes each subsequent one to fall in order

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='20' width='110' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='55' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>East Asian</text><text x='55' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Crisis</text><text x='118' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>></text><rect x='126' y='20' width='110' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='181' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Sub-prime</text><text x='181' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Crisis (2007)</text><text x='244' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>></text><rect x='252' y='20' width='110' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='307' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Global Fin.</text><text x='307' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Crisis (2008)</text><text x='370' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>></text><rect x='378' y='20' width='110' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='433' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Banking</text><text x='433' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Crisis (2008)</text><text x='495' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>></text><rect x='503' y='20' width='52' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='529' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Lehman</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A left-to-right arrow chain showing: East Asian Crisis -> Sub-prime Crisis (2007) -> Global Financial Crisis (2008) -> Banking Crisis (2008) -> Lehman collapse

given.aim: Arrange the four financial events in the correct causal and chronological order as described in the passage

terms.meaning: Created excess liquidity in financial markets; this is the root cause mentioned in the passage
terms.example: Excess money from Asia flowed into US and global credit markets
terms.connects: Must be first in the sequence; it caused sub-prime lending conditions

terms.meaning: Emerged in USA in 2007 due to excess liquidity from East Asian crisis; risky loans given to unqualified borrowers
terms.example: Banks gave mortgages to people who could not afford them
terms.connects: Second in the sequence; it led to the global financial crisis

terms.meaning: Resulted from the sub-prime lending crisis; considered the most serious worldwide financial crisis
terms.example: 2008 global recession affecting economies around the world
terms.connects: Third in the sequence; it encompassed the banking crisis

terms.meaning: The banking crisis occurred within the global financial crisis; Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008
terms.example: Lehman Brothers filing for bankruptcy in September 2008
terms.connects: Fourth and last event in the described sequence

given.plan: Trace the causal chain given in the passage: excess liquidity from East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis (USA 2007) -> global financial crisis (2008) -> banking crisis (collapse of Lehman Brothers 2008)

steps.title: Identify the starting event
steps.apply: The passage says sub-prime lending arises because of excess liquidity following the East Asian crisis. So the East Asian crisis is the earliest event.
steps.note: The phrase 'following the East Asian crisis' places it before all other events in the chain

steps.title: Place the sub-prime lending crisis
steps.apply: The sub-prime lending crisis began in USA in 2007 as a direct consequence of excess liquidity. It comes second: East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis.
steps.note: Year 2007 confirms it is after the East Asian crisis but before the 2008 events

steps.title: Place the global financial crisis
steps.apply: The passage states the global financial crisis started with the sub-prime lending crisis. It comes third: ... -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis.
steps.note: The global financial crisis is described as the overarching event triggered by the sub-prime crisis

steps.title: Place the banking crisis last
steps.apply: The sub-prime lending crisis led to the banking crisis in 2008 (Lehman Brothers collapse). The banking crisis is part of the global financial crisis, occurring last in the chain.
steps.note: Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008, within the global financial crisis context

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Correct sequence: East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis. This matches option A exactly.
steps.note: Options B, C, D all place events in incorrect causal orders that contradict the passage

solution.result: Option A is correct: East Asian crisis -> sub-prime lending crisis -> global financial crisis -> banking crisis.

questions.formula_note: This is a reading comprehension sequence question; no mathematical formulas are needed. The correct order is determined by tracing the causal chain explicitly described in the passage.
